perturbation.original_text: Notices will be sent to a party at its address set forth in this Agreement or such other address as that party may specify in writing pursuant to this Section.
perturbation.changed_text: Notices will be sent to a party at its address set forth in this Agreement or such other address as that party may specify verbally.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring address changes to be specified verbally contradicts the Statute of Frauds, which requires certain contracts and modifications to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Statute of Frauds requires that agreements modifying a contract for the sale of goods worth $500 or more be in writing. Allowing verbal address changes (which would impact notice) introduces an element of uncertainty and potential unenforceability, as it would be difficult to prove the changed address and therefore whether proper notice was given, should a dispute arise under the contract.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a lo
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by allowing verbal address changes, directly contradicts the Statute of Frauds as outlined in California Civil Code section 1624(a). The original text required written notice for address changes, maintaining a record. The changed text introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability, because verbal agreements are more difficult to prove than written ones. Snippet 1 states that certain agreements are invalid unless they are in writing. The modification in the contract creates an inconsistency with this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: If Excite fails to deliver the indicated number of impressions required during any annual period, Client may suspend (but not eliminate) its payments specified in Section 7 for a maximum of sixty (60) days (the "Make-Good Period) during which Excite will deliver the shortfall of such impressions.
perturbation.changed_text: If Excite fails to deliver the indicated number of impressions required during any annual period, Client may suspend (but not eliminate) its payments specified in Section 7 for a maximum of fifteen (15) days (the "Make-Good Period) during which Excite will deliver the shortfall of such impressions.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the make-good period to 15 days could be considered commercially unreasonable, potentially violating California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly specifying a time frame, the covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance and enforcement of their contracts. Reducing the make-good period to 15 days may be considered unreasonable if it does not provide Excite with a sufficient opportunity to remedy the shortfall, effectively depriving them of the benefit of the bargain. This is particularly true given the complexities of ad delivery and the potential for unforeseen circumstances, making it harder to resolve technical issues.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation [1201 - 1206] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 1201. (a) Unless the context otherwise requires, words or phrases defined in this section, or in the additional definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof, have the meanings stated. (b) Subject to definitions contained in other divisions of this code that apply to particular divisions or chapters thereof: (1) “Action,” in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceeding in which rights are determined. (2) “Aggrieved party” means a party entitled to pursue a remedy. (3) “Agreement,” as distinguished from “contract,” means the bargain of the parties in fact, as found in their language or inferred from other circumstances, including course of performance, course of dealing, or usage of trade as provided in Section 1303. (4) “Bank” means a person engaged in the business of banking, and includes a savings bank, savings and loan association, credit union, and trust company. (5) “Bearer” means a person in possessio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CHAPTER 2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS AND PRINCIPLES OF INTERPRETATION - Sections 1201-1210 :: California Commercial Code :: 2005 California Code :: California Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the California Code 2024 California Code 2023 California Code 2022 California Code 2005 California Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 California Commercial Code Sections 1201-1210 CHAPTER 2. GENERAL DEFINITIONS AND PRINCIPLES OF INTERPRETATION COMMERCIAL CODE SECTION 1201-1210 1201. The following definitions apply for purposes of this code, subject to additional definitions contained in the subsequent divisions of this code that apply to specific divisions or chapters thereof, and unless the context otherwise requires: (1) "Action," in the sense of a judicial proceeding, includes recoupment, counterclaim, setoff, suit in equity, and any other proceedings in which rights are determined. (2) "Aggrieved party" means a party entitled to resort to a remedy. (3) "Agreement" means the bargain of the parties in fact as found in their language or by implication from other circumstances, including course of dealing, usage of trade, and course of performance as provided in this code (Sections 1205, 2208, and 10207). Whether an agreement has legal consequences is determined by the provisions of this code, if applicable, and otherwise by the law of contracts (Section 1103). (Compare "contract.") (4) "Bank" means any person engaged in the business of banking. (5) "Bearer" means the person in possession of an instrument, document of title, or certificated security payable to bearer or endorsed in blank. (6) "Bill of lading" means a document evidencing the receipt of goods for shipment issued by a person engaged in the business of transporting or forwarding goods, and that, by its terms, evidences the intention of the issuer that the person entitled under the document (Section 7403(4)) has the right to receive, hold, and dispose of the document and the goods it covers. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the 'Make-Good Period' from sixty (60) days to fifteen (15) days. The Law Explanation argues this might deprive Excite of the benefit of the bargain, potentially violating the covenant of good faith and fair dealing by being commercially unreasonable. The perturbation explanation also supports this argument. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state the specifics of the "covenant of good faith and fair dealing", they do define "agreement" as 'the bargain of the parties in fact'. Reducing the make-good period to 15 days could undermine this 'bargain' if it doesn't allow sufficient time to resolve shortfalls. The change introduces a direct contradiction by potentially making the contract commercially unreasonable, affecting Excite's ability to fulfill the agreement.
